Items.Title: 在 Cosmos DB (.NET SDK) 中接收多种文档类型 - 来自评论 | Receiving Multiple Document Types in Cosmos DB (.NET SDK) - From Review
Items.Description:
这是来自我们新博客的交叉帖子。 任何未来与开发相关的文章也将在此处交叉发布给开发社区 💪

容器、分区键、点读取、查询、请求单元。 这就是 Cosmos DB 的生命，它非常棒，直到您需要了解一些东西。

如果您最终确实想了解有关 Cosmos DB 的具体信息，那么您可能会像所有其他开发人员一样去 Google 搜索它。 然后你会发现自己在 Stack Overflow 上阅读被误导的问题并收到有问题的答案。 然后您会后悔无法轻松浏览 Microsoft 的大多数优秀但有时缺乏的文档。

在这里，我将带您了解有关 Cosmos DB 的一些关键事项，并根据 Google Overlords 的工作情况，希望能回答人们正在搜索的一些问题。

它很长，所以这里有一个 TLDR：在使用 Cosmos 容器和多种文档类型时，不要使用dynamic也不要使用JsonConvert.DeserialiseObject() 。

首先介绍一下这篇文章的写作背景。 在[插入我们的下一个企业名称]，我们将 Cosmos 用于我们架构的某些部分，这些部分从 NoSQL 和高效操作中受益匪浅，包括但不限于审计、配置、高可用性内部服务、事件流和基本键值类型存储要求。

昨晚深夜，我正在审查我们一项使用 Cosmos 作为后备存储的服务中的关键路径，并注意到它实际上在过去几周内对其工作方式进行了一些根本性的更改。 此代码在单个容器中存储和接收多种不同类型的文档（如果不鼓励的话，这是一种完全有效的做法）。

该代码负责从 Cosmos 中的容器读取不同的文档类型，并将它们正确地转换为它们的实际类型。

以下是不同版本的内容：

  1. 一次只获取一个预先定义的项目。 简单、有效、快速、强类型。 这使用了 Cosmos SDK 中的ReadItemAsync<T> 。
  2. 获取所有文档并将它们作为要序列化回 JSON 的object返回给 API。 这使用带有分区键的查询迭代器和类似SELECT * FROM c的查询。 所以做所有的下一个。
  3. 使用dynamic和 Newtonsoft 的 JsonConvert 反序列化.ToString()的结果
  4. 使用dynamic和 System.Text.Json 的 JsonSerializer 反序列化.ToString()的结果

它们都是〜可接受的〜方法，但我对它们中的任何一个都不满意。

首先，我想说我是仅在两种情况下使用dynamic的忠实拥护者。 第一种是给同事们打气，然后为了好玩而惊呼“DYNAMIC ALL THE WAY”。 第二种情况是当你真的不知道发生了什么，而某些事情实际上可以是任何事情，你需要保持灵活性，通常是在与第三方或其他非输入语言交流时，我们也不太关心很多。

当我在我们正在运行的实际生产代码中以及在每秒将调用多次的路径中看到dynamic时，我有点困惑并进行了更深入的挖掘。

让我们了解这是从哪里来的。 这里有 2 个值得注意的 Stack Overflow 问题，它们正在做我们正在做的事情：

  • 他通常不会用我所见的代码来回答，更多的是关于理论和最佳实践，但这次他包含了一些代码，并指出在这种情况下使用dynamic是一种“典型模式”。
  • 还有更多这些基本上都在说同样的事情，这些只是我在写作时发现的前两个。

    这些答案都做了一件奇怪的事情，获取动态对象，检查文档上的类型属性，然后将其JsonConvert为我们想要的类型。 很公平。

    但是吗？ 让我们来看看。

    我在这里创建了一个基准来测试不同的方法，有 2 个文档要测试，一个是 1.8KB，另一个是 2.1KB。 微不足道。 这也在 .NET 6 和 7 上进行了测试，因为我们根据环境在生产中使用它们。 我在具有 16GB RAM 和大量其他打开的东西的 M1 Pro 上运行这些，所以 YMMV。

    另请注意，Newtonsoft.Json 是 Cosmos SDK v3 的默认（反）序列化程序。 这是基准代码。

     Type1 t1Out; Type2 t2Out; foreach (var obj in DynamicObjects) { if (obj.type == "type_1") { t1Out = JsonConvert.DeserializeObject<Type1>(obj.ToString()); } else if (obj.type == "type_2") { t2Out = JsonConvert.DeserializeObject<Type2>(obj.ToString()); } }
     | Method | Job | Runtime | Mean | Error | StdDev | Gen0 | Gen1 | Allocated | |----------------------------- |--------- |--------- |---------:|---------:|---------:|--------:|-------:|----------:| | Dynamic_ToString_Deserialize | .NET 6.0 | .NET 6.0 | 33.63 us | 0.087 us | 0.077 us | 21.3623 | - | 43.7 KB | | Dynamic_ToString_Deserialize | .NET 7.0 | .NET 7.0 | 27.36 us | 0.381 us | 0.356 us | 7.1106 | 0.2441 | 43.7 KB |

    好吧。 让我们看看我们如何通过使用 System.Text.Json 对其进行反序列化来对其进行一些改进（这实际上是我审查过的最新版本）。

    新代码：

     Type1 t1Out; Type2 t2Out; foreach (var obj in DynamicObjects) { if (obj.type == "type_1") { t1Out = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<Type1>(obj.ToString()); } else if (obj.type == "type_2") { t2Out = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<Type2>(obj.ToString()); } }

    结果：

     | Method | Job | Runtime | Mean | Error | StdDev | Gen0 | Gen1 | Allocated | |-------------------------------------------- |--------- |--------- |---------:|---------:|---------:|--------:|-------:|----------:| | Dynamic_ToString_SystemTextJson_Deserialize | .NET 6.0 | .NET 6.0 | 14.96 us | 0.038 us | 0.033 us | 13.4583 | - | 27.48 KB | | Dynamic_ToString_SystemTextJson_Deserialize | .NET 7.0 | .NET 7.0 | 13.53 us | 0.130 us | 0.109 us | 4.4708 | 0.1373 | 27.48 KB |

    各方面都更好！ 它速度更快，分配的内存减少了 42%。 赢了！ 我们已经击败了 Stack Overflow 的答案。

    所以这就是留下并提交的代码，如果我不是学究的话，它会投入生产。 但我是。

    我们知道 Cosmos DB 本身以及 SDK 实际上并不关心我们在那里存储什么类型，只要它们满足某些条件（实际上拥有分区键实际上是唯一的条件，将生成一个 ID，并且分区键可能是那个 ID！），所以对象本身实际上不是dynamic ，它是某种东西。

    是的。 某物。 在 SDK 的 V2 中，我相信这是Document ，因为它在其他一些 Stack Overflow 答案中被引用，尽管我没有亲眼看到它被使用，所以无法发表评论。

    让我们用它来工作，而不是使用dynamic ，据说它是性能世界中万恶之源。 它们都是使用 JObject.Parse 创建的（这是 Cosmos 内部所做的），我们现在才输入。

    这给了我们更多的类型安全性。 我多说一点，因为我们仍然假设obj["type"]在那里并且它是一个字符串。 这很安全。

     Type1 t1Out; Type2 t2Out; foreach (var obj in Objects) { var type = (string)obj["type"]; if (type == "type_1") { t1Out = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<Type1>(obj.ToString()); // And the equivalent JsonConvert in another benchmark } else if (type == "type_2") { t2Out = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<Type2>(obj.ToString()); } }

    太好了，我们在内存分配上减少了几个字节。 完毕！ 我们通过使用JObject获得了类型安全（ish，kinda，sorta）并且我们节省了一些时间。

    显然，我在开玩笑。 让我们看看JObject实际上是什么。

    表示一个 JSON 对象。

    有帮助。

    无论如何，为了避免咆哮，它基本上是一个代表其中文档的解析对象。

    它实际上保存了 ToString() 刚刚返回的原始文档 JSON 吗？ 当然不是，为什么会这样？ 让我们看看我们的分配实际上来自哪里。

     | Method | Job | Runtime | Mean | Error | StdDev | Gen0 | Gen1 | Allocated | |----------------- |--------- |--------- |---------:|----------:|----------:|--------:|-------:|----------:| | JObject_ToString | .NET 6.0 | .NET 6.0 | 9.795 us | 0.0190 us | 0.0159 us | 13.3209 | - | 27.23 KB | | JObject_ToString | .NET 7.0 | .NET 7.0 | 8.681 us | 0.1725 us | 0.2634 us | 4.4403 | 0.0916 | 27.23 KB |

    当我们调用 JObject.ToString() 时，我们将对象重新序列化回 JSON。 实际上，这就是 Stack Overflow 问题的答案所做的：

    1. 让 Cosmos 从数据库本身加载文档 blob
    2. 让 Newtonsoft.Json 将其从 JSON 反序列化为 JObject
    3. 使用.ToString() ，将其从 JObject 序列化回 JSON
    4. 将它反序列化（使用 Newtonsoft 或在我们的例子中使用 System.Text.Json）回到我们的类型

    在这种情况下，我们两次获得了相同的 JSON，并且也解析了两次，从而导致所有分配。

    我们还了解到 System.Text.Json 实际上非常棒，导致在.ToString()之上几乎没有额外分配。 当然 Newtonsoft.Json 基本上是它的两倍，但我们知道无论如何它都无法与 System.Text.Json 竞争简单文档（反）序列化。

    话虽如此，要使用 System.Text.Json，我们仍然必须调用此 .ToString() 方法。 一定有更好的方法！

    好吧，因为JObject也是JToken ，这意味着我们可以访问ToObject<T> 。 我们仍将生活在 Newtonsoft.Json 世界中，但我们已经在那里了。

    所以我们开始：

     Type1 t1Out; Type2 t2Out; foreach (var obj in Objects) { var type = obj["type"].ToString(); // or type = (string)obj["type"] // or type = obj["type"].Value<string>() // it doesn't matter if (type == "type_1") { t1Out = obj.ToObject<Type1>(); } else if (type == "type_2") { t2Out = obj.ToObject<Type2>(); } }
     | Method | Job | Runtime | Mean | Error | StdDev | Gen0 | Allocated | |-------------------------- |--------- |--------- |---------:|---------:|---------:|-------:|----------:| | JObject_Cast_ToObject | .NET 6.0 | .NET 6.0 | 16.36 us | 0.046 us | 0.043 us | 1.6174 | 3.32 KB | | JObject_ToString_ToObject | .NET 6.0 | .NET 6.0 | 16.22 us | 0.082 us | 0.072 us | 1.6174 | 3.32 KB | | JObject_Value_ToObject | .NET 6.0 | .NET 6.0 | 16.42 us | 0.051 us | 0.048 us | 1.6174 | 3.32 KB | | Dynamic_ToObject | .NET 6.0 | .NET 6.0 | 16.80 us | 0.019 us | 0.017 us | 1.6479 | 3.39 KB | | JObject_Cast_ToObject | .NET 7.0 | .NET 7.0 | 12.52 us | 0.030 us | 0.027 us | 0.5341 | 3.32 KB | | JObject_ToString_ToObject | .NET 7.0 | .NET 7.0 | 12.03 us | 0.018 us | 0.015 us | 0.5341 | 3.32 KB | | JObject_Value_ToObject | .NET 7.0 | .NET 7.0 | 11.86 us | 0.016 us | 0.015 us | 0.5341 | 3.32 KB | | Dynamic_ToObject | .NET 7.0 | .NET 7.0 | 12.33 us | 0.034 us | 0.031 us | 0.5493 | 3.39 KB |

    一致的结果，没有不必要的（反）序列化，它和使用 System.Text.Json 解析它一样快（好吧，当然比它快一点）。 时间彼此如此接近以至于无关紧要，但内存分配足够重要，足以在我们快速连续地一遍又一遍地调用此代码时产生影响。

    为了更好地衡量，我还通过dynamic基准测试返回了调用ToObject<T>()的方法，差异也可以忽略不计，但是当我们知道它是什么时为什么还要使用它呢？

    因此，我们有它。 我们不是调用.ToString() （序列化）然后反序列化结果，而是简单地完成更少的工作并将其简单地映射到我们的目标类型。

    在这种情况下， dynamic实际上并不是那么邪恶（尽管它仍然比显式更邪恶）。

    对于仍然在这里并且足够敏锐地意识到我们在 Cosmos 中对该类型进行了 Point Read 而不是使用查询来取回所有文档的任何人来说，这是一个小奖励。 通过使用 Point Reads，我们将从一开始就拥有强类型，并且可以说代码会稍微简单一些。

    粗略地说，点读 1 x 1KB 文件就是 1 RU。 查询在 Cosmos 端激活查询引擎，基本成本约为 2.8 RU。

    假设我们有 4 种文档类型需要阅读，每种大约 2KB，我们可以假设一次 Point Read 将花费 2RU x 4 = 8 RU。 因此，我们使用 8 RU 并花费大约 32 毫秒从 Cosmos 加载文档，包括解析以加载这 4 个文档。

    然而，我们只加载了 4 个文档，需要知道的重要一点是奇怪的是，基于查询的 RU 不随文档大小缩放。 这似乎对 Azure 的底线不利，但对我们来说却很好！

    运行SELECT * FROM c并返回这 4 个文档时，查询成本为 2.93 RU，耗时 22 毫秒。 我们将成本削减一半，时间削减三分之一。

    如果您有类似的设置，如果您还没有查询，请尝试一下。 只需确保为查询指定分区键，因为您不希望进行任何跨分区查询。

    供您参考，它看起来有点像这样：

     var query = new QueryDefinition("SELECT * FROM c"); var iterator = container.GetItemQueryIterator<JObject>(query, requestOptions: new QueryRequestOptions { PartitionKey = partitionKey });

    快乐宇宙！ 🪐 🪐



原文:

This is a cross-post from our new blog. Any future development-related articles will also be cross-posted here for the dev community 💪

Containers, Partition Keys, Point Reads, Queries, Request Units. That's the life of Cosmos DB and it's pretty great until you need to know something.

If you do end up wanting to know something specific about Cosmos DB then like every other developer you're probably going to Google it. Then you'll find yourself on Stack Overflow reading misguided questions and receiving questionable answers. Then you'll regret not being able to easily navigate Microsoft's mostly-excellent, but sometimes-lacking, documentation.

Here I'll take you through a few key things to know about Cosmos DB and depending on the Google Overlords doing their job, hopefully answer a few questions that people are searching for.

It's pretty long so here's a TLDR: Don't use dynamic and don't use JsonConvert.DeserialiseObject() when working with a Cosmos container and multiple document types.

A little backstory first as to what prompted this article. At [insert our next venture name] we use Cosmos for some parts of our architecture that heavily benefits from NoSQL and efficient operations including but not limited to auditing, configurations, highly available internal services, event streams and basic key-value type storage requirements.

Late last night I was reviewing a critical path in one of our services that uses Cosmos as a backing store and noticed that it had actually undergone several fundamental changes to how it worked in the last couple of weeks. This code was storing and receiving multiple different types of documents in a single Container (which is a perfectly valid, if not encouraged practice).

The code was responsible for reading different document types from a Container in Cosmos and correctly getting them to their actual types.

Here's what the different versions were:

  1. Only get a single item at a time with the defined up front. Simple, effective, fast, strongly typed. This used ReadItemAsync<T> from the Cosmos SDK.
  2. Get all of the documents and return them to the API as object to be serialized back to JSON. This uses a Query Iterator with a query like SELECT * FROM c, with a partition key. So do all of the next ones.
  3. Use dynamic and Newtonsoft's JsonConvert to Deserialize the result of .ToString()
  4. Use dynamic and System.Text.Json's JsonSerializer to Deserialize the result of .ToString()

They're all ~acceptable~ ways of doing this yet I wasn't happy with any of them.

I want to start by saying that I'm a huge fan of using dynamic in only 2 scenarios. The first is to wind up colleagues and exclaim "DYNAMIC ALL THE WAY" just for fun. The second is when you truly don't have a clue what's going on and something could literally be anything and you need to be flexible, generally when communicating with third parties or other languages that aren't typed and we don't really care too much.

When I saw dynamic in actual production code that we're running and in a path that will be called many times per second I was a little confused and dug deeper.

Let's understand where this came from. Here's 2 notable Stack Overflow questions that are doing what we're doing:

  • The first which includes a sample from Mark Brown, someone I consider to be the CosmosDB God. He doesn't generally answer with code from what I've seen, more about the theory and best practices, but this time he included some code as well as stating that using dynamic in this scenario is a "typical pattern" to use.
  • The second has answers from Iahsrah and Nick Chapsas (a pretty cool YouTube developer) which both also reference dynamic as the way to go.

There are more of these that all basically say the same thing, these are just the first 2 I found whilst writing.

These answers both do a curious thing, take the dynamic object, check the type property on the document, and then JsonConvert it to the type we want. Fair enough.

But is it? Let's take a look.

Here I've created a benchmark to test out different methods, there's 2 documents to test with, 1 is 1.8KB and the other is 2.1KB. Negligible. This is also tested on both .NET 6 and 7 as we use both in production depending on the environment. I ran these on an M1 Pro with 16GB RAM and a tonne of other stuff open, so YMMV.

Note also that Newtonsoft.Json is the default (de)serializer for the Cosmos SDK v3. v4 which seems to never be coming (it hasn't been touched for years) does use System.Text.Json but for now we're stuck.

Here's the benchmark code.

Type1 t1Out;
Type2 t2Out;
foreach (var obj in DynamicObjects)
{ if (obj.type == "type_1") { t1Out = JsonConvert.DeserializeObject<Type1>(obj.ToString()); } else if (obj.type == "type_2") { t2Out = JsonConvert.DeserializeObject<Type2>(obj.ToString()); }
}

|                       Method |      Job |  Runtime |     Mean |    Error |   StdDev |    Gen0 |   Gen1 | Allocated |
|----------------------------- |--------- |--------- |---------:|---------:|---------:|--------:|-------:|----------:|
| Dynamic_ToString_Deserialize | .NET 6.0 | .NET 6.0 | 33.63 us | 0.087 us | 0.077 us | 21.3623 |      - |   43.7 KB |
| Dynamic_ToString_Deserialize | .NET 7.0 | .NET 7.0 | 27.36 us | 0.381 us | 0.356 us |  7.1106 | 0.2441 |   43.7 KB |

Alright. 43.7KB memory, around 30 nanoseconds.

Let's see how we improved it a little (this is essentially the latest version I had reviewed) by using System.Text.Json to deserialize it.

New code:

Type1 t1Out;
Type2 t2Out;
foreach (var obj in DynamicObjects)
{ if (obj.type == "type_1") { t1Out = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<Type1>(obj.ToString()); } else if (obj.type == "type_2") { t2Out = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<Type2>(obj.ToString()); }
}

and the results:

|                                      Method |      Job |  Runtime |     Mean |    Error |   StdDev |    Gen0 |   Gen1 | Allocated |
|-------------------------------------------- |--------- |--------- |---------:|---------:|---------:|--------:|-------:|----------:|
| Dynamic_ToString_SystemTextJson_Deserialize | .NET 6.0 | .NET 6.0 | 14.96 us | 0.038 us | 0.033 us | 13.4583 |      - |  27.48 KB |
| Dynamic_ToString_SystemTextJson_Deserialize | .NET 7.0 | .NET 7.0 | 13.53 us | 0.130 us | 0.109 us |  4.4708 | 0.1373 |  27.48 KB |

Better in every way! It's faster and allocates 42% less memory. Winning! We've beat out the Stack Overflow answers already.

So that is the code that was left and committed and would have made it out in to production if I wasn't a pedant. But I am.

We know that Cosmos DB itself as well as the SDK don't actually care what types we're storing up there as long as they meet some criteria (actually having a partition key is actually the only criteria, an ID will be generated, and the partition key could be that ID!), so the object itself is not actually dynamic, it's something.

Yup. Something. In V2 of the SDK, I believe this was Document as it's referenced in some other Stack Overflow answers though I haven't personally seen it used so can't comment.

Let's work with that, and instead of using dynamic which is the root of all evil in the performance world, supposedly. Instead of DynamicObjects (which is what it says on the tin), Objects is the same thing but as a JObject. They were both created using JObject.Parse (which is what Cosmos does internally), we're just typed now.

This gives us a little more type safety. I say a little more because we're still going to just assume obj["type"] is there and it's a string. Which is pretty safe.

Type1 t1Out;
Type2 t2Out;
foreach (var obj in Objects)
{ var type = (string)obj["type"]; if (type == "type_1") { t1Out = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<Type1>(obj.ToString()); // And the equivalent JsonConvert in another benchmark } else if (type == "type_2") { t2Out = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<Type2>(obj.ToString()); }
}

(and the JsonConvert equivalent for good comparative measure).

|                                           Method |      Job |  Runtime |     Mean |    Error |   StdDev |    Gen0 |   Gen1 | Allocated |
|------------------------------------------------- |--------- |--------- |---------:|---------:|---------:|--------:|-------:|----------:|
|                JObject_Cast_ToString_Deserialize | .NET 6.0 | .NET 6.0 | 34.14 us | 0.288 us | 0.240 us | 21.3623 |      - |  43.63 KB |
| JObject_Cast_ToString_SystemTextJson_Deserialize | .NET 6.0 | .NET 6.0 | 15.33 us | 0.304 us | 0.338 us | 13.4277 |      - |  27.41 KB |
|                JObject_Cast_ToString_Deserialize | .NET 7.0 | .NET 7.0 | 27.39 us | 0.424 us | 0.397 us |  7.1106 | 0.2136 |  43.63 KB |
| JObject_Cast_ToString_SystemTextJson_Deserialize | .NET 7.0 | .NET 7.0 | 13.02 us | 0.254 us | 0.250 us |  4.4708 | 0.1221 |  27.41 KB |

Excellent, we've shaved off a few bytes on memory allocation. Done! We have type-safety(ish, kinda, sorta) from using JObject and we shaved off some time.

I'm joking, obviously. Let's look at what a JObject actually is.

Represents a JSON object.

Helpful.

Anyway to save a rant, it's basically a parsed object representing the document that was in it.

Does it actually hold the original document JSON though which ToString() is just returning? Of course not, why would it? Let's see where our allocations are actually coming from.

|           Method |      Job |  Runtime |     Mean |     Error |    StdDev |    Gen0 |   Gen1 | Allocated |
|----------------- |--------- |--------- |---------:|----------:|----------:|--------:|-------:|----------:|
| JObject_ToString | .NET 6.0 | .NET 6.0 | 9.795 us | 0.0190 us | 0.0159 us | 13.3209 |      - |  27.23 KB |
| JObject_ToString | .NET 7.0 | .NET 7.0 | 8.681 us | 0.1725 us | 0.2634 us |  4.4403 | 0.0916 |  27.23 KB |

When we're calling JObject.ToString() we are reserializing the object back to JSON. In effect, this is what the answers to the Stack Overflow questions are doing:

  1. Let Cosmos load the document blob from the database itself
  2. Let Newtonsoft.Json Deserialize it from JSON to a JObject
  3. Using .ToString(), serialize it back from a JObject to JSON
  4. Deserialize it (using Newtonsoft or in our case, System.Text.Json) back to our type

We got the same JSON twice in this case, and parsed it twice too resulting in all the allocations.

We also learned that System.Text.Json is actually super awesome, resulting in virtually no extra allocations above the .ToString(). Of course Newtonsoft.Json basically doubles it, but we knew that it was nowhere near in competition with System.Text.Json for simple document (de)serialization anyway.

With that said, to use System.Text.Json we still have to call this .ToString() method. There has to be a better way!

Well there is, since a JObject is also a JToken, it means we can access ToObject<T>. We'll live in the Newtonsoft.Json world still, but we're already there anyway.

So here we go:

Type1 t1Out;
Type2 t2Out;
foreach (var obj in Objects)
{ var type = obj["type"].ToString(); // or type = (string)obj["type"] // or type = obj["type"].Value<string>() // it doesn't matter if (type == "type_1") { t1Out = obj.ToObject<Type1>(); } else if (type == "type_2") { t2Out = obj.ToObject<Type2>(); }
}

|                    Method |      Job |  Runtime |     Mean |    Error |   StdDev |   Gen0 | Allocated |
|-------------------------- |--------- |--------- |---------:|---------:|---------:|-------:|----------:|
|     JObject_Cast_ToObject | .NET 6.0 | .NET 6.0 | 16.36 us | 0.046 us | 0.043 us | 1.6174 |   3.32 KB |
| JObject_ToString_ToObject | .NET 6.0 | .NET 6.0 | 16.22 us | 0.082 us | 0.072 us | 1.6174 |   3.32 KB |
|    JObject_Value_ToObject | .NET 6.0 | .NET 6.0 | 16.42 us | 0.051 us | 0.048 us | 1.6174 |   3.32 KB |
|          Dynamic_ToObject | .NET 6.0 | .NET 6.0 | 16.80 us | 0.019 us | 0.017 us | 1.6479 |   3.39 KB |
|     JObject_Cast_ToObject | .NET 7.0 | .NET 7.0 | 12.52 us | 0.030 us | 0.027 us | 0.5341 |   3.32 KB |
| JObject_ToString_ToObject | .NET 7.0 | .NET 7.0 | 12.03 us | 0.018 us | 0.015 us | 0.5341 |   3.32 KB |
|    JObject_Value_ToObject | .NET 7.0 | .NET 7.0 | 11.86 us | 0.016 us | 0.015 us | 0.5341 |   3.32 KB |
|          Dynamic_ToObject | .NET 7.0 | .NET 7.0 | 12.33 us | 0.034 us | 0.031 us | 0.5493 |   3.39 KB |

Consistent results, no unnecessary (de)serializing and it's just as fast as (well, slightly faster than, of course) using System.Text.Json to parse it back. The times are all so close to one another that it doesn't matter much, but the memory allocations are significant enough to make a difference when we're calling this code over and over again in quick succession.

For good measure I also through the dynamic benchmark back in just calling ToObject<T>() with also negligible differences, but why use it when we know what it is?

So there we have it. Rather than calling .ToString() (serialize) and then deserializing the result from, we simply let much less of the work be done and simply map it to our target type.

Also dynamic is not actually that evil in this case (though it's still more evil than being explicit).

A small bonus for any of you that are still here and are eagle eyed enough to realise that we did a Point Read in Cosmos on the type rather than using the query to get back all of the documents. By using Point Reads we would have the strong type from the start and arguably slightly simpler code.

Cosmos DB bills on Request Units (or RUs). Roughly, point reading 1 x 1KB document is 1 RU. 1 x 100KB document is 100 RU. Queries activate the Query Engine at the Cosmos Side and have a base cost of around 2.8 RU.

Let's say we had 4 document types that we wanted to read, each at roughly 2KB, we can assume that a Point Read would cost us 2RU x 4 = 8 RU. Point Reads are super quick, and we see them returning in around 8ms generally which is astonishing. With that we're using 8 RU and spending around 32ms loading in the documents from Cosmos inclusive of the parsing to load in these 4 documents.

However, we're only loading in 4 documents and an important thing to know is that strangely query-based RUs don't scale with document size. This seems bad for Azure's bottom line, but great for us!

When running SELECT * FROM c and returning those 4 documents, the query costs 2.93 RU and takes 22ms. We're slashing costs in half, and time by a third.

If you have a similar set up to this give it a try if you're not already querying. Just make sure that you specify the Partition Key for the query as you don't want any cross partition queries going on.

For your reference it'll look a little something like this:

var query = new QueryDefinition("SELECT * FROM c");
var iterator = container.GetItemQueryIterator<JObject>(query, requestOptions: new QueryRequestOptions
{
    PartitionKey = partitionKey
});

Happy Cosmosing! 🪐
Items.Author: rudi_visser

Items.Title: Kubernetes-101：命名空间 | Kubernetes-101: Namespaces
Items.Description:
到目前为止，在我们进入 Kubernetes 世界的旅程中，我们已经在集群中创建了资源，但对命名空间一无所知。 什么是命名空间？ 简短的回答是，它是我们 Kubernetes 集群中的一个逻辑隔间，我们可以在其中部署我们的 Kubernetes 对象。 在下图中，我们有两个命名空间， Namespace 1和Namespace 2 ，每个命名空间都包含一个具有三个 Pod 的 Deployment。

您可以将命名空间想象成办公室中的小隔间。 每个隔间都有自己的桌子和电脑。 每个隔间的居民仍然可以看到其他隔间的居民并与之互动，但每张桌子和电脑都在自己的隔间内。 这不是一个完美的类比，但它是我此时此刻使用的类比！

我们将在以下部分更深入地探讨命名空间的主题。

命名空间

大多数资源都在命名空间范围内。 默认命名空间名为default 。 当我们创建资源（如 Pod、Deployments、Services）时，除非我们明确指定不同的命名空间，否则它们将被放置在default命名空间中。 我们可以像使用任何其他资源一样使用kubectl创建命名空间。 在以下部分中，我们将探讨如何使用命令式方法和声明式方法来完成此操作。

命令式地使用命名空间

在我们创建一个新的命名空间之前，让我们看看我们的集群中有哪些可用的命名空间。 我们可以使用kubectl get列出现有的命名空间：

 $ kubectl get namespaces NAME STATUS AGE default Active 7d kube-node-lease Active 7d kube-public Active 7d kube-system Active 7d

显然我的集群中有四个现有的命名空间。 您在此列表中看到的命名空间数量可能会有所不同，具体取决于您使用的 Kubernetes 集群类型。 在我的例子中，我使用了一个已经运行了一周的本地 Minikube 集群。 名称以kube-开头的命名空间通常供 Kubernetes 内部使用，因此您应该避免将自己的资源放入这些命名空间中。

像往常一样，我们可以通过使用namespaces的缩写形式ns来缩短前面的命令，所以前面的命令可以写成kubectl get ns 。

短暂的kubectl间歇时间：有没有办法列出所有可用的对象短名称，例如ns用于namespaces和po用于pods ？ 就在这里！ 运行kubectl api-resources命令：

 $ kubectl api-resources NAME SHORTNAMES APIVERSION NAMESPACED KIND configmaps cm v1 true ConfigMap namespaces ns v1 false Namespace nodes no v1 false Node persistentvolumeclaims pvc v1 true PersistentVolumeClaim persistentvolumes pv v1 false PersistentVolume pods po v1 true Pod secrets v1 true Secret serviceaccounts sa v1 true ServiceAccount services svc v1 true Service daemonsets ds apps/v1 true DaemonSet deployments deploy apps/v1 true Deployment replicasets rs apps/v1 true ReplicaSet statefulsets sts apps/v1 true StatefulSet horizontalpodautoscalers hpa autoscaling/v2 true HorizontalPodAutoscaler cronjobs cj batch/v1 true CronJob jobs batch/v1 true Job ingresses ing networking.k8s.io/v1 true Ingress roles rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1 true Role

我删除了一些输出行，因为它们很多。 我们可以看到有一个名为SHORTNAMES的列，它显示了给定资源类型的缩写形式。 我们还可以看到一些资源（例如secrets和jobs ）没有缩写形式。 此命令的另一个有趣的输出是名为NAMESPACED列。 此列指示资源是否在命名空间范围内。 许多资源都限定在命名空间范围内，但也有一些不是，最明显的是命名空间资源本身。

需要注意的是， kubectl api-resources的输出显示了当前集群中可用的资源类型。 两个不同集群的输出可能不同，具体取决于可用的资源类型。

现在是时候创建我们的第一个命名空间了。 创建命名空间的必要方法是运行kubectl create ：

 $ kubectl create namespace custom-namespace namespace/custom-namespace created

如果我们再次列出所有命名空间，我们可以看到名为custom-namespace的新命名空间确实已创建：

 $ kubectl get namespaces NAME STATUS AGE custom-namespace Active 57s default Active 7d kube-node-lease Active 7d kube-public Active 7d kube-system Active 7d

我们可以使用kubectl describe获取有关特定命名空间的更多详细信息：

 $ kubectl describe namespace custom-namespace Name: custom-namespace Labels: kubernetes.io/metadata.name=custom-namespace Annotations: <none> Status: Active No resource quota. No LimitRange resource.

输出提示我们可以将资源配额和 LimitRanges 应用于命名空间。 这两个控制资源（CPU、内存）可以被命名空间中的资源分配和消耗。 我们不会在本文中详细介绍这些细节，但我们可能会在以后的文章中重新讨论它。

我们可以使用kubectl delete删除命名空间：

 $ kubectl delete namespace custom-namespace namespace "custom-namespace" deleted

如果我们删除包含资源的命名空间会怎样？ 所有这些资源也将被删除。

以声明方式创建命名空间

命名空间只是 Kubernetes 中的一种资源，就像我们目前看到的任何其他资源1一样，因此我们当然可以为我们的命名空间编写一个 Kubernetes 清单：

 # namespace.yaml apiVersion: v1 kind: Namespace metadata: name: custom-namespace

简短而简单！ 像往常一样，我们可以使用kubectl apply这个清单：

 $ kubectl apply -f namespace.yaml namespace/custom-namespace created

我们可以使用kubectl delete再次删除命名空间：

 $ kubectl delete -f namespace.yaml namespace "custom-namespace" deleted

在自定义命名空间中创建资源

我们如何将命名空间用于其他资源？ 我们如何在自定义命名空间中运行kubectl get 、 kubectl describe 、 kubectl apply和其他kubectl命令？ 这就是--namespace或-n标志派上用场的地方。 我们将在本节中看到一些如何使用它的示例。

为了演示如何在自定义命名空间中创建部署，我们将使用以下部署清单：

 # deployment.yaml apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: nginx-deployment labels: app: nginx spec: replicas: 3 selector: matchLabels: app: nginx template: metadata: labels: app: nginx spec: containers: - name: nginx image: nginx:latest ports: - containerPort: 80

首先，我使用kubectl create创建一个新的命名空间：

 $ kubectl create namespace web namespace/web created

然后我运行kubectl apply在这个命名空间中创建我的部署，我记得添加-n标志：

 $ kubectl apply -n web -f deployment.yaml deployment.apps/nginx-deployment created

如果我想在此命名空间中列出部署，我同样将-n标志添加到kubectl get命令：

 $ kubectl get deployments -n web NAME READY UP-TO-DATE AVAILABLE AGE nginx-deployment 3/3 3 3 60s

为了演示如果我删除包含资源的命名空间会发生什么，我将这样做：

 $ kubectl delete namespace web namespace "web" deleted $ kubectl get deployments -n web No resources found in web namespace.

作为本节的最后一个示例，让我们看看是否可以使用我们在上一篇文章中看到的内容来创建命名空间和部署。 让我们首先为我们的命名空间和部署2创建一个复合清单文件：

 # application.yaml --- apiVersion: v1 kind: Namespace metadata: name: web --- apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: nginx-deployment namespace: web # <--- added this labels: app: nginx spec: replicas: 3 selector: matchLabels: app: nginx template: metadata: labels: app: nginx spec: containers: - name: nginx image: nginx:latest ports: - containerPort: 80

请注意我是如何将.metadata.namespace添加到我的部署清单中的。 这很重要，否则它将被部署到default命名空间。

我们使用kubectl apply创建这两个资源：

 $ kubectl apply -f application.yaml namespace/web created deployment.apps/nginx-deployment created

有效！ 现在我们开始了解如何将我们需要的一切定义为一个内聚的 YAML 文件包。 我们正在构建最终使用 Helm 3等工具打包我们的应用程序。

列出所有命名空间的资源

如果我们有数百个命名空间并且我们希望在一个巨大的列表中查看所有这些命名空间中的所有 Pod 怎么办？ 为此，您可以添加--all-namespaces或-A标志。 下面显示了一个例子：

 $ kubectl get pods -A NAMESPACE NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE default nginx-deployment-6fdcdfb74d-hjp7m 1/1 Running 2 (9h ago) 33h default nginx-deployment-6fdcdfb74d-lm6z6 1/1 Running 2 (9h ago) 33h default nginx-deployment-6fdcdfb74d-s77pg 1/1 Running 2 (9h ago) 33h kube-system coredns-565d847f94-nllvw 1/1 Running 4 (9h ago) 7d6h kube-system etcd-minikube 1/1 Running 5 (9h ago) 7d6h kube-system kube-apiserver-minikube 1/1 Running 5 (9h ago) 7d6h kube-system kube-controller-manager-minikube 1/1 Running 5 (9h ago) 7d6h kube-system kube-proxy-2jcqr 1/1 Running 4 (9h ago) 7d6h kube-system kube-scheduler-minikube 1/1 Running 5 (9h ago) 7d6h kube-system storage-provisioner 1/1 Running 8 (9h ago) 7d6h

输出显示了我自己的 Pod（在default命名空间中）和一些系统 Pod（在kube-system命名空间中）的混合。

概括

我们现在知道什么是命名空间，也知道如何使用kubectl来处理我们的命名空间。 当有许多应用程序将在同一集群中运行时，命名空间非常有用。 我们还可以使用命名空间执行本文中未提及的其他操作，包括限制命名空间之间的网络流量以及将资源配额应用于命名空间。 但我们在本文中看到的内容足以让我们走得更远！

在下一篇文章中，我们将了解ConfigMaps和Secrets 。 这些资源可用于为 Pod 提供配置（例如环境变量）和秘密值。

  1. 在之前的文章中，我将 Deployments/ReplicaSets/Jobs 等归类为工作负载资源，将 Service 归类为网络资源。 我们应该如何对命名空间进行分类？ 在官方文档中，集群资源下列出了一个命名空间。 有关命名空间的 API 参考，请参阅kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubernetes… 。 如果您错过了，在上一篇文章中我展示了我们可以将所有清单添加到一个文件中，只要我们用包含三个破折号 ( --- ) 的行分隔每个清单。 在 Helm 中，您以 Helm Chart 的形式打包您的应用程序，它由 Kubernetes 清单的模板版本组成。 我们将在以后的文章中看到 Helm，但现在您可以在helm.sh/查看官方 Helm 页面。 ↩ ↩



原文:

So far on our journey into the world of Kubernetes we have created resources in our cluster without knowing anything about Namespaces. What is a Namespace? The short answer is that it is a logical compartment in our Kubernetes cluster where we can deploy our Kubernetes objects. In the following illustration we have two Namespaces, Namespace 1 and Namespace 2, each containing a Deployment with three Pods.

You could think about Namespaces like cubicles in an office. Each cubicle contains its own desk and computer. The inhabitants of each cubicle can still see and interact with inhabitants of other cubicles, but each desk and computer stays within its own cubicle boundary. It is not a perfect analogy, but it is the one I went with right here and now!

We will explore the topic of Namespaces a bit closer in the following sections.

Namespaces

Most resources are scoped to a Namespace. The default Namespace is named default. When we create resources, such as Pods, Deployments, Services, they are placed into the default Namespace unless we explicitly specify a different Namespace. We can create Namespaces using kubectl like any other resource. In the following sections we will explore how this is done using an imperative approach as well as a declarative approach.

Imperatively working with Namespaces

Before we create a new Namespace, let's see what Namespaces are available in our cluster. We can list existing Namespaces with kubectl get:

$ kubectl get namespaces

NAME STATUS AGE
default Active 7d
kube-node-lease Active 7d
kube-public Active 7d
kube-system Active 7d

Apparently I have four existing Namespaces in my cluster. The number of Namespaces you see in this list could vary depending on what type of Kubernetes Cluster you are using. In my case I use a local Minikube cluster that has been running for a week. Namespaces with names starting with kube- are generally for internal use by Kubernetes, so you should avoid placing your own resources into these Namespaces.

As usual we could shorten the previous command a bit by using the short form of namespaces which is ns, so the previous command can be written as kubectl get ns.

Time for a brief kubectl intermission: Is there a way to list all available short names for objects, such as ns for namespaces and po for pods? Yes there is! Run the kubectl api-resources command:

$ kubectl api-resources

NAME SHORTNAMES APIVERSION NAMESPACED KIND
configmaps cm v1 true ConfigMap
namespaces ns v1 false Namespace
nodes no v1 false Node
persistentvolumeclaims pvc v1 true PersistentVolumeClaim
persistentvolumes pv v1 false PersistentVolume
pods po v1 true Pod
secrets v1 true Secret
serviceaccounts sa v1 true ServiceAccount
services svc v1 true Service
daemonsets ds apps/v1 true DaemonSet
deployments deploy apps/v1 true Deployment
replicasets rs apps/v1 true ReplicaSet
statefulsets sts apps/v1 true StatefulSet
horizontalpodautoscalers hpa autoscaling/v2 true HorizontalPodAutoscaler
cronjobs cj batch/v1 true CronJob
jobs batch/v1 true Job
ingresses ing networking.k8s.io/v1 true Ingress
roles rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1 true Role

I have removed some of the output rows because there were a lot of them. We can see that there is a column named SHORTNAMES that shows what the short form for a given resource type is. We can also see that some resources (e.g. secrets and jobs) do not have a short form. Another interesting output from this command is the column named NAMESPACED. This column indicates whether the resource is scoped to a Namespace or not. Many resources are scoped to a Namespace, but a few are not, most obviously the Namespace resource itself.

It should be noted that the output from kubectl api-resources shows what resource types are available in the current cluster. The output could be different for two different clusters, depending on what resource types are available.

Now it is time to create our first Namespace. The imperative way to create a Namespace is to run kubectl create:

$ kubectl create namespace custom-namespace

namespace/custom-namespace created

If we list all our Namespaces again we can see that our new Namespace named custom-namespace was indeed created:

$ kubectl get namespaces

NAME STATUS AGE
custom-namespace Active 57s
default Active 7d
kube-node-lease Active 7d
kube-public Active 7d
kube-system Active 7d

We can get more details about a specific Namespace with kubectl describe:

$ kubectl describe namespace custom-namespace

Name: custom-namespace
Labels: kubernetes.io/metadata.name=custom-namespace
Annotations: <none>
Status: Active No resource quota. No LimitRange resource.

The output hints that we could apply resource quotas and LimitRanges to a Namespace. Both of those control resources (CPU, memory) that can be allocated and consumed by resources in the Namespace. We won't go into those details in this article, but we might revisit it in a future article.

We can delete a Namespace using kubectl delete:

$ kubectl delete namespace custom-namespace

namespace "custom-namespace" deleted

What happens if we delete a Namespace that contains resources? All of those resources will be deleted as well.

Declaratively creating a Namespace

A Namespace is just a resource in Kubernetes like any other resource we have seen so far1, so of course we can write a Kubernetes manifest for our Namespace:

# namespace.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata: name: custom-namespace

Short and simple! As usual we can apply this manifest with kubectl apply:

$ kubectl apply -f namespace.yaml

namespace/custom-namespace created

And we can delete the Namespace again using kubectl delete:

$ kubectl delete -f namespace.yaml

namespace "custom-namespace" deleted

Creating resources in a custom Namespace

How do we use our Namespace for our other resources? How can we run kubectl get, kubectl describe, kubectl apply and other kubectl commands in our custom Namespace? This is where the --namespace or -n flag comes in handy. We'll see some examples of how it is used in this section.

To demonstrate how to create a Deployment in a custom Namespace we will use the following Deployment manifest:

# deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata: name: nginx-deployment labels: app: nginx
spec: replicas: 3 selector: matchLabels: app: nginx template: metadata: labels: app: nginx spec: containers: - name: nginx image: nginx:latest ports: - containerPort: 80

First I create a new Namespace with kubectl create:

$ kubectl create namespace web

namespace/web created

Then I run kubectl apply to create my Deployment in this Namespace, and I remember to add the -n flag:

$ kubectl apply -n web -f deployment.yaml

deployment.apps/nginx-deployment created

If I want to list Deployments in this Namespace I likewise add the -n flag to the kubectl get command:

$ kubectl get deployments -n web

NAME READY UP-TO-DATE AVAILABLE AGE
nginx-deployment 3/3 3 3 60s

To demonstrate what happens if I delete the Namespace that contains resources I will do just that:

$ kubectl delete namespace web

namespace "web" deleted $ kubectl get deployments -n web

No resources found in web namespace.

As a last example in this section, let's see if we can create our Namespace along with our Deployment using what we saw in the previous article. Let us first create a composite manifest file for our Namespace and our Deployment2:

# application.yaml
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata: name: web
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata: name: nginx-deployment namespace: web # <--- added this labels: app: nginx
spec: replicas: 3 selector: matchLabels: app: nginx template: metadata: labels: app: nginx spec: containers: - name: nginx image: nginx:latest ports: - containerPort: 80

Note how I have added .metadata.namespace to my Deployment manifest. This is important because otherwise it will be deployed to the default Namespace.

We create both resources using kubectl apply:

$ kubectl apply -f application.yaml

namespace/web created
deployment.apps/nginx-deployment created

It worked! Now we are starting to see how we can define everything we need as one cohesive package of YAML files. We are building up to eventually packaging our application using a tool such as Helm3.

Listing resources for all Namespaces

What if we have hundreds of Namespaces and we wish to view all Pods in all of these Namespaces in one giant list? To do that you can add the --all-namespaces or -A flag. An example of this is shown below:

$ kubectl get pods -A

NAMESPACE NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE
default nginx-deployment-6fdcdfb74d-hjp7m 1/1 Running 2 (9h ago) 33h
default nginx-deployment-6fdcdfb74d-lm6z6 1/1 Running 2 (9h ago) 33h
default nginx-deployment-6fdcdfb74d-s77pg 1/1 Running 2 (9h ago) 33h
kube-system coredns-565d847f94-nllvw 1/1 Running 4 (9h ago) 7d6h
kube-system etcd-minikube 1/1 Running 5 (9h ago) 7d6h
kube-system kube-apiserver-minikube 1/1 Running 5 (9h ago) 7d6h
kube-system kube-controller-manager-minikube 1/1 Running 5 (9h ago) 7d6h
kube-system kube-proxy-2jcqr 1/1 Running 4 (9h ago) 7d6h
kube-system kube-scheduler-minikube 1/1 Running 5 (9h ago) 7d6h
kube-system storage-provisioner 1/1 Running 8 (9h ago) 7d6h

The output shows a mix of my own Pods (in the default Namespace) and a few system Pods (in the kube-system Namespace).

Summary

We now know what a Namespace is, and we know how to work with our Namespaces using kubectl. Namespaces are very useful when there are many applications that will run in the same cluster. There are additional things we can do with Namespaces that we have not seen in this article, including restricting network traffic between Namespaces and applying resource quotas to a Namespace. But what we have seen in this article is enough to get us far!

In the next article we will take a look at ConfigMaps and Secrets. These resources can be used to provide configuration (e.g. environment variables) and secret values to Pods.

  1. In previous articles I classified Deployments/ReplicaSets/Jobs/etc as workload resources, and I classified a Service as a network resource. How should we classify a Namespace? In the official documentation a Namespace is listed under cluster resources. See kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubernetes… for the API-reference for Namespaces. ↩

  2. In case you missed it, in the previous article I showed that we could add all our manifests in a single file as long as we separate each manifest by a line containing three dashes (---). ↩

  3. Helm is a package manager for Kubernetes applications. In Helm you package your application in the form of a Helm Chart, that consists of templated versions of your Kubernetes manifests. We will see Helm in future articles, but for now you can check out the official Helm page at helm.sh/. ↩

Items.Title: 哪个更好：Metaverse 或虚拟现实？ | Which is better: the Metaverse or Virtual Reality?
Items.Description:
虚拟世界或替代现实的概念已经存在了几十年，但随着技术的快速进步，这些想法变得越来越有形。 近年来受到广泛关注的两个术语是“虚拟世界”和“虚拟现实”。 尽管它们看起来相似，但实际上它们彼此完全不同。
它是一个完全身临其境的、持久的三维虚拟世界，这意味着即使用户注销，它也会继续存在和发展。 相反，虚拟现实 (VR) 是指计算机生成的环境模拟，用户可以通过特殊的硬件和软件与之交互。 虽然虚拟现实可以创造高度逼真和身临其境的体验，但它不一定涉及共享空间或持久世界。
元宇宙和虚拟现实都有其独特的优点和缺点，两者之间的选择将取决于预期目的和预期结果。

元宇宙
多年来，从尼尔斯蒂芬森的雪崩到欧内斯特克莱恩的头号玩家，元宇宙一直是科幻小说和流行文化中的一个流行概念。 然而，随着虚拟现实和增强现实的兴起，元宇宙正在成为许多行业更具可行性和吸引力的前景。

它可以为人们提供一个平台，让他们以目前不可能的方式相互联系和互动，从而带来令人兴奋的新社交、教育和商业机会。

例如，在教育领域，元宇宙可以实现模拟真实场景和环境的沉浸式交互式学习体验，从而使教育更具吸引力和有效性。 在医疗保健领域，元宇宙可以让医疗专业人员在全球范围内协作并分享知识和专业知识，从而改善患者的治疗效果并推进医学研究。

然而，虚拟世界也带来了一些挑战，特别是在隐私、安全和治理方面。 随着虚拟世界变得更加复杂和有影响力，将需要制定明确而稳健的政策来确保用户安全和保护。 此外，随着虚拟世界变得越来越商业化，将需要制定法规来防止垄断并确保公平竞争。

虚拟现实
另一方面，虚拟现实已经在包括游戏、娱乐和培训在内的多个行业中占据重要地位。 例如，VR 可用于培训飞行员、外科医生和军事人员，使他们能够在高压力情况下获得经验，而不会给自己或他人带来任何风险。

此外，虚拟现实在娱乐和游戏方面具有巨大潜力，可为用户提供全新水平的沉浸感和互动性。 从身临其境的故事讲述到互动游戏，VR 可以彻底改变娱乐业，创造出以前不可能的全新体验。

然而，VR 也有其局限性，尤其是在可访问性和成本方面。 此外，并不是每个人都对 VR 提供的沉浸感和感官刺激感到满意，这对一些用户来说可能是压倒性的，甚至是令人作呕的。

结论
总之，虚拟世界和虚拟现实为技术和社会的未来提供了独特而令人兴奋的可能性。 虚拟世界为人们提供了一个共享的、持久的虚拟空间来进行交互和协作，而虚拟现实则为培训、娱乐和教育提供了沉浸式、逼真的模拟。 两者之间的选择将取决于预期目的。

作者- Adarsh Sinha



原文:

The concept of a virtual world or an alternate reality has been around for decades, but with the rapid advances in technology, these ideas are becoming increasingly tangible. Two terms that have gained significant attention in recent years are the "metaverse" and "virtual reality." While they may seem similar, they are, in fact, quite different from one another.
The metaverse refers to a collective virtual shared space, typically designed to facilitate interaction between users from all over the world.

It is a fully immersive, three-dimensional virtual world that is persistent, meaning it continues to exist and evolve even when users log off. In contrast, virtual reality (VR) refers to a computer-generated simulation of an environment that users can interact with through special hardware and software. While virtual reality can create highly realistic and immersive experiences, it does not necessarily involve a shared space or persistent world.
Both the metaverse and virtual reality have their unique benefits and drawbacks, and the choice between the two will depend on the intended purpose and desired outcomes.

The Metaverse
The metaverse has been a popular concept in science fiction and popular culture for many years, from Neal Stephenson's Snow Crash to Ernest Cline's Ready Player One. However, with the rise of virtual and augmented reality, the metaverse is becoming a more feasible and attractive prospect for many industries.

One of the most significant benefits of the metaverse is its potential for creating shared spaces for people all over the world to interact and collaborate in real time. It could provide a platform for people to connect and engage with one another in ways that are currently impossible, allowing for new and exciting social, educational, and commercial opportunities.

For example, in education, the metaverse could enable immersive, interactive learning experiences that simulate real-world scenarios and environments, making education more engaging and effective. In healthcare, the metaverse could allow medical professionals to collaborate and share knowledge and expertise across the globe, improving patient outcomes and advancing medical research.

However, the metaverse also poses several challenges, particularly concerning privacy, security, and governance. As the metaverse becomes more complex and influential, there will need to be clear and robust policies in place to ensure user safety and protection. Moreover, as the metaverse becomes increasingly commercialized, there will need to be regulations to prevent monopolies and ensure fair competition.

Virtual Reality
Virtual reality, on the other hand, has already gained a significant grip in several industries, including gaming, entertainment, and training. VR allows users to enter and interact with a fully immersive digital environment, creating a sense of presence and immersion that is unparalleled by any other medium.

One of the most significant benefits of VR is its ability to simulate real-world scenarios and environments, providing valuable training and educational opportunities. For example, VR can be used to train pilots, surgeons, and military personnel, allowing them to gain experience in high-stress situations without any risk to themselves or others.

Moreover, VR has significant potential in entertainment and gaming, providing users with an entirely new level of immersion and interactivity. From immersive storytelling to interactive games, VR can revolutionize the entertainment industry, creating entirely new experiences that were previously impossible.

However, VR also has its limitations, particularly when it comes to accessibility and cost. VR hardware and software can be expensive, making it difficult for many people to access. Moreover, not everyone is comfortable with the level of immersion and sensory stimulation that VR provides, which can be overwhelming or even nauseating for some users.

Conclusion
In conclusion, the metaverse and virtual reality offer distinct and exciting possibilities for the future of technology and society. While the metaverse provides a shared, persistent virtual space for people to interact and collaborate, virtual reality offers immersive, realistic simulations for training, entertainment, and education. The choice between the two will depend on the intended purpose.

Written By- Adarsh Sinha

Items.Description:
伊利亚·N·齐金

发表于 4 月 21 日

如何在 Rails 应用程序中改进控制器的简单案例

首先我想检查一个应用程序的#root路由

我去config/routes.rb看看

Rails.application.routes.draw do root to: 'root#index' end

步骤 2. 检查控制器

我访问控制器app/controllers/root_controller.rb

 class RootController < ActionController::Base def index @mailto = "hello@company.com" @blogs = Blog .published .limit(10) end end

第三步：如何改进

我看到了一种改进控制器的方法。 我想移动常量中的数字和字符串。

规则：不要在代码中保留Magic numbers 。 总是移动它常量！

 class RootController < ActionController::Base MAIN_EMAIL = "hello@company.com" INDEX_BLOGS_COUNT = 10 def index @mailto = MAIN_EMAIL @blogs = Blog .published .limit(INDEX_BLOGS_COUNT) end end

步骤 4. 添加测试

在我的应用程序测试环境已经设置。 我只会稍微改进现有的测试。

 require 'rails_helper' RSpec.describe RootController type: :controller do describe 'Constants' do it "has expected INDEX_BLOGS_COUNT" do expect(RootController::INDEX_BLOGS_COUNT).to eq 10 end it "has expected MAIN_EMAIL" do expect(RootController::MAIN_EMAIL).to eq("hello@company.com") end end describe '#index' do let!(:published_blogs) { create_list :blogs, 3, :published } before do get :index end it do expect(response).to render_template(:index) end it do expect(assigns(:blogs).map(&:id)).to match_array(published_blogs.map(&:id)) end it { expect(assigns(:mailto)).to eq RootController::MAIN_EMAIL } end end

步骤 5. 运行测试用例

bundle exec rspec spec/controllers/root_controller_spec.rb

这就对了！

Simple case how to improve your controller in a Rails application

Step 1. Route to the main page

First I want to check #root route of an App

I go to config/routes.rb and see

Rails.application.routes.draw do root to: 'root#index'
end

Step 2. Checking the controller

class RootController < ActionController::Base def index @mailto = "hello@company.com" @blogs = Blog .published .limit(10) end
end

Step 3. How to Improve

I see a way to improve the controller. I want to move numbers and strings in constants.

Rule: Do not keep Magic numbers in your code. Always move it constants!

class RootController < ActionController::Base MAIN_EMAIL = "hello@company.com" INDEX_BLOGS_COUNT = 10 def index @mailto = MAIN_EMAIL @blogs = Blog .published .limit(INDEX_BLOGS_COUNT) end
end

Step 4. Adding tests

In my app test environment is already set up. I will just improve existed tests a bit.

require 'rails_helper' RSpec.describe RootController type: :controller do describe 'Constants' do it "has expected INDEX_BLOGS_COUNT" do expect(RootController::INDEX_BLOGS_COUNT).to eq 10 end it "has expected MAIN_EMAIL" do expect(RootController::MAIN_EMAIL).to eq("hello@company.com") end end describe '#index' do let!(:published_blogs) { create_list :blogs, 3, :published } before do get :index end it do expect(response).to render_template(:index) end it do expect(assigns(:blogs).map(&:id)).to match_array(published_blogs.map(&:id)) end it { expect(assigns(:mailto)).to eq RootController::MAIN_EMAIL } end
end

Step 5. Running the test case 

bundle exec rspec spec/controllers/root_controller_spec.rb

That is it!
Happy Coding!

您是否正在寻找加速 ASP.NET 应用程序的方法？ 如果是这样，你并不孤单。 提高应用程序性能是许多开发人员面临的共同挑战。 我们将探索一些提高 ASP.NET 应用程序性能的提示和技术。

  • 使用缓存： ASP.NET 提供了多种缓存机制，包括内存缓存、输出缓存和数据缓存。
  • 优化图像：使用 Photoshop 或在线图像压缩器等工具优化图像。
  • 使用内容分发网络 (CDN)：通过使用 CDN，您可以提高应用程序的性能并减少服务器上的负载。

  • 优化数据库查询：使用 Entity Framework 或 LINQ to SQL 编写优化查询。
  • 使用 Profiler：您可以使用 ANTS Performance Profiler 或 dotTrace 等分析工具来衡量应用程序的性能并确定需要改进的地方。
  • 使用更快的 Web 服务器：将 IIS 配置为使用 HTTP 压缩和缓存来减少服务器上的负载。
  • 使用压缩：使用 ASP.NET 中的 GZipStream 或 DeflateStream 类来压缩响应数据。

Are you looking for ways to speed up your ASP.NET application? If so, you're not alone. Improving application performance is a common challenge for many developers. We'll explore some tips and techniques for boosting your ASP.NET application's performance.

  • Minify CSS and JavaScript: Use tools like YUI Compressor or Google Closure Compiler to minify your files.
  • Use Asynchronous Programming: Use async/await keywords in C# to perform asynchronous operations.
  • Optimize Database Queries: Use Entity Framework or LINQ to SQL to write optimized queries.
  • Use a Profiler: You can use profiling tools like ANTS Performance Profiler or dotTrace to measure your application's performance and identify areas that need improvement.
  • Use a Faster Web Server: Configure IIS to use HTTP compression and caching to reduce the load on your server.
  • Use Compression: Use GZipStream or DeflateStream classes in ASP.NET to compress your response data.

Items.Title: Next.js 基础知识 | Next.js fundamentals
Items.Description:
哈桑·哈比卜·塔希尔

发表于 4 月 21 日

静态站点生成 (SSG)

静态生成描述了在构建时编译和呈现网站的过程。输出是一堆静态文件，包括 HTML 文件本身和 JavaScript 和 CSS 等资产。 使用静态生成，像 Next.js 这样的工具会尝试像在浏览器中一样呈现该页面，但在编译时。 这使我们能够在第一次加载时提供全部内容。

如果您只使用* getStaticProps *来获取数据，Next.js 将在构建时获取该数据，为您留下一个完全静态的页面。


自动代码拆分

当用户加载您的应用程序时，Next.js 仅发送初始路由所需的代码。
然后它为每个页面生成单独的 JavaScript 块，以便每个页面只加载它需要的代码。

总体而言，自动代码拆分是通过减少每个页面的初始加载时间和内存占用来优化 Next.js 应用程序性能的强大工具。

 // pages/index.js export default function Home() { return ( <div> <h1>Welcome to my Next.js app!</h1> <p>This is a simple example of a Next.js application.</p> </div> ) }

例如，index.js 文件对应于应用程序的根 URL（即 /）。 下面是在 Next.js 中使用 API 路由的示例：

 // pages/api/hello.js export default function handler(req, res) { res.status(200).json({ message: 'Hello from the API!' }) }

动态路由

我们有一个博客文章的动态路径。 该路由在 pages/posts 目录中名为 [id].js 的文件中定义。 当用户导航到匹配该路由的 URL 时，Next.js 会自动将请求路由到该页面并将 id 的值作为查询参数传递。 我们使用 Next.js 中的 useRouter 钩子来访问路由器对象，然后我们从查询对象中提取 id 查询参数。

动态路由是 Next.js 的一项强大功能，可让您构建灵活且动态的 Web 应用程序。

布局

这可以帮助您避免重复代码并提高可维护性。

 // components/Layout.js import Head from 'next/head' const Layout = ({ children }) => { return ( <div> <Head> <title>My Next.js App</title> <link rel="icon" href="/favicon.ico" /> </Head> <header> <nav> {/* navigation links */} </nav> </header> <main> {children} </main> <footer> {/* footer content */} </footer> </div> ) } export default Layout

我们定义了一个 Layout 组件来包裹每个页面的内容。 该组件包括页眉、导航链接、主要内容和页脚。 我们还包括来自 Next.js 的 Head 组件，用于设置页面标题和图标。

 // pages/index.js import Layout from '../components/Layout' export default function Home() { return ( <Layout> <h1>Welcome to my Next.js app!</h1> <p>This is a simple example of a Next.js application.</p> </Layout> ) }

我们导入 Layout 组件并将我们的页面内容（标题和段落）包装在其中。 这确保页眉、导航、主要内容和页脚在应用程序的所有页面中保持一致。

下面是一个示例，说明如何在 Next.js 中创建 API 路由。

 // pages/api/hello.js export default function handler(req, res) { res.status(200).json({ message: 'Hello from the API!' }) }

我们已经创建了一个 API 路由，它使用包含消息的 JSON 对象进行响应。 该路由在 pages/api 中名为 hello.js 的文件中定义。
当用户向 /api/hello 发出 GET 请求时，Next.js 会自动将请求路由到此页面并调用处理函数。 处理函数有两个参数：req（传入的 HTTP 请求）和 res（服务器响应）。

HassanHabibTahir

Posted on Apr 21

Next.js allows you to perform server-side rendering of your React components,which means that your pages are pre-rendered on the server and delivered as static HTML to the client.The HTML page is generated on the server for each request. React 18 and Next 12 introduce React server Components, Server completely rendered on the server and do not require client-side rendering.it can degrade performance if it is heavy.

export async function getServerSideProps(context) {
  const { id } = context.params
  const res = await fetch(`https://api.example.com/posts/${id}`)
  const post = await res.json()

  return {
    props: {
      post
    }
  }
}

function Post({ post }) {
  return (
    <div>
      <h1>{post.title}</h1>
      <p>{post.body}</p>
    </div>
  )
}

export default Post

Static site generation (SSG)

Static Generation describes the process of compiling and rendering a website at build time.The output is a bunch of static files, including the HTML file itself and assets like JavaScript and CSS. With Static Generation, tools like Next.js try to render that page mostly like it would in the browser but at compile time. This gives us the ability to serve the entire content on first load.

If you only use *getStaticProps * to fetch data, Next.js will fetch that data at build time, leaving you with a completely static page.


*How to build a Next.js app
* Now that we have our application available, let’s try to build it. npm run build 
import path from "path";
import fs from "fs/promises";
import { Fragment } from "react";
import { useRouter } from "next/router";
function ProductDetailPage(props: any) {
  const router = useRouter();
  const { loadedProduct } = props;
  if (router?.isFallback) {
    return <h2> page Not Found!</h2>;
  }
  return (
    <Fragment>
      <h1>this is page</h1>
      <h1>{loadedProduct?.title}</h1>
      <h1>{loadedProduct?.description}</h1>
    </Fragment>
  );
}

async function getData() {
  const filePath = path.join(process.cwd(), "data", "dummy-data.json");
  const jsonData = await fs.readFile(filePath, "utf-8");
  const data = JSON.parse(jsonData);
  return data;
}

export async function getStaticProps(context: any) {
  const { params } = context;
  const productId = params.pid;
  const data = await getData();
  const product = data.products.find(
    (product: any) => product.id === productId
  );
  if (product) {
    return {
      props: {
        loadedProduct: product || null,
      },
    };
  }
}

export async function getStaticPaths() {
  const data = await getData();
  const ids = data.products.map((product: any) => product.id);
  const params = ids.map((id: string) => ({ params: { pid: id } }));
  return {
    paths: params,
    fallback: false,
  };
}

export default ProductDetailPage;

Automatic code splitting

Next.js splits your JavaScript into separate chunks for each route. When users load your application, Next.js only sends the code needed for the initial route.
Next.js automatically performs code splitting by analyzing the application code and determining which parts of it are needed for each page. It then generates separate chunks of JavaScript for each page, so that each page only loads the code it needs.

Overall, automatic code splitting is a powerful tool for optimizing the performance of a Next.js application, by reducing the initial load time and memory footprint of each page.

// pages/index.js

export default function Home() {
  return (
    <div>
      <h1>Welcome to my Next.js app!</h1>
      <p>This is a simple example of a Next.js application.</p>
    </div>
  )
}

Next.js provides a file-based routing system, which means that each file in the pages directory corresponds to a URL route in the application. For example, the index.js file corresponds to the root URL of the application (i.e. /). Here's an example of using an API route in Next.js:

// pages/api/hello.js

export default function handler(req, res) {
  res.status(200).json({ message: 'Hello from the API!' })
}

Dynamic routing

Next.js supports dynamic routing, which allows you to create routes with parameters that can be accessed by pages and components.

// pages/posts/[id].js

import { useRouter } from 'next/router'

export default function Post() {
  const router = useRouter()
  const { id } = router.query

  return (
    <div>
      <h1>Post {id}</h1>
      <p>This is the post with ID {id}</p>
    </div>
  )
}

we have a dynamic route for blog posts. The route is defined in a file called [id].js inside the pages/posts directory. The brackets around id indicate that this part of the URL is dynamic, and can be replaced with any value. When a user navigates to a URL that matches this route, Next.js will automatically route the request to this page and pass the value of id as a query parameter. We use the useRouter hook from Next.js to get access to the router object, and then we extract the id query parameter from the query object.

Dynamic routing is a powerful feature of Next.js that allows you to build flexible and dynamic web applications.

Layouts

Next.js provides a way to define reusable layouts that can be used across multiple pages. This can help you avoid duplicating code and improve maintainability.

// components/Layout.js

import Head from 'next/head'

const Layout = ({ children }) => {
  return (
    <div>
      <Head>
        <title>My Next.js App</title>
        <link rel="icon" href="/favicon.ico" />
      </Head>
      <header>
        <nav>
          {/* navigation links */}
        </nav>
      </header>
      <main>
        {children}
      </main>
      <footer>
        {/* footer content */}
      </footer>
    </div>
  )
}

export default Layout

we define a Layout component that wraps around the content of each page. The component includes a header, navigation links, main content, and a footer. We also include a Head component from Next.js that sets the page title and favicon.

// pages/index.js

import Layout from '../components/Layout'

export default function Home() {
  return (
    <Layout>
      <h1>Welcome to my Next.js app!</h1>
      <p>This is a simple example of a Next.js application.</p>
    </Layout>
  )
}

we import the Layout component and wrap our page content (a heading and a paragraph) inside it. This ensures that the header, navigation, main content, and footer are consistent across all pages of the application.

API routes

API routes in Next.js provide a simple and easy way to create serverless functions that can handle API requests. Here's an example of how you can create an API route in Next.js.

// pages/api/hello.js
export default function handler(req, res) {
  res.status(200).json({ message: 'Hello from the API!' })
}

we have created an API route that responds with a JSON object containing a message. The route is defined in a file called hello.js inside the pages/api.
When a user makes a GET request to /api/hello, Next.js will automatically route the request to this page and invoke the handler function. The handler function takes two arguments: req (the incoming HTTP request) and res (the server response).

Items.Title: 可观察性 | 简化的 | Observability | Simplified
Items.Description:
可观察性是我们开发人员圈子中的一个常用术语； 通常与监控和警报相结合。 许多流行的工具声称可以端到端地解决您的问题，并且围绕开源技术和协议进行了大量交流。 本文试图简化其中一些术语以及可观察性的真正工作原理。

什么是可观察性？

可观察性是拥有有关系统的数据的实践，可以帮助您了解未知事物。 它不涉及您的指标仪表板（即监控）或您设置的警报。 检测和收集数据的过程使您能够观察软件系统的行为方式，了解它们的健康状况并收集有关它们如何工作的详细知识，这就是可观察性。

有 3 种常见类型的数据源（称为遥测数据）可帮助您揭开真相：

如果您是开发人员，那么在测试代码时添加的第一件事就是日志。 它们可以是系统生成的（例如由 nginx）或手动生成的，并且可以包含各种有助于了解有关代码执行的重要信息的数据。

它们是量化软件的某个行为方面的数值，这些数值保存在时间序列存储中以供在一段时间内查看。 大多数软件都会发出指标，无论是在 pod 上运行的服务还是 k8s 集群本身。

换句话说，吞吐量（每分钟请求数）或每分钟 API 调用的平均响应时间是您熟悉的一些指标，并且必须在仪表板中注意到这些指标。

您可以将跟踪视为一种特殊形式的日志，旨在提供有关您的“请求”所采取的步骤集的详细信息。 它将您的整个执行分成更小的块，包括代码级逻辑、数据库查询和下游调用。 这些执行（称为“跨度”）很容易通过它们的名称和前缀来识别。 如果您的团队已经设置了跟踪，您可能会看到的常用名称：

  1. 数据存储 - 数据库查询和连接处理步骤

  2. 外部 - 通过网络在您的服务外部进行的调用
    函数 - 当前程序中的代码执行 根据您的情况，可以出现其他跨度名称

    仪器代理。

什么是监控？

监控是您使用遥测数据设置仪表板和指标可视化的部分，您已经知道需要跟踪这些指标以在任何时间点查看系统的运行状况。 可观察性意味着拥有这样的数据，即使您不知道需要跟踪什么，您也可以足够深入地调查您的系统。

这是如何运作的？

下面是一个示例流程，展示了可观察性在集成到您的微服务架构中时的样子。

通常，它涉及向现有代码添加一小段代码/程序（检测代理）。

好的，但我需要知道它是如何工作的吗？ 不，不是真的。 您可以决定继续使用商业工具，您需要做的就是按照几行说明进行设置。 上面提到的所有步骤都由他们处理，因此细节被抽象出来，您可以直接开始监控您的系统。

警告：随着系统规模的扩大，商业工具的成本将开始下降，您可能会考虑转向 OSS。

奖金部分：

在过去的几年中，术语 o11y 开始因可观察性一词而流行起来（例如 The event o11yfest ）。

现在你说怎么办？ 查找此代码的输出以了解操作方法：

 def word_encoder(word): word = word.replace(" ","") #removing spaces mid_char_count = len(word) - 2 encoding = word[0].lower() + str(mid_char_count) + word[-1].lower() print(encoding) return encoding word_encoder("observability")

您是否在想“这种灵感来自哪里？” 尝试找到这些函数调用的输出，您就会知道答案:)

 word_encoder("kubernetes") word_encoder("Andreessen Horowitz")

有趣的事实：这些词是“ numeronym ”

如果您遇到任何其他需要简化的行话，请在评论中提及！

原文:

Observability is a common term thrown around in our developer circles; often coupled with monitoring & alerting. A lot of popular tools claim to be solving your problems end-to-end and a lot of exchanges go on around open source technologies and protocols around this. This article tries to simplify some of these terms and how observability really works.

What is Observability?

Observability is the practice of having data about your system that can help you know the unknown. It doesn’t refer to your metrics dashboards (that is monitoring) or to the alerts you set up. The process of instrumenting and collecting data that enables you to observe how your software systems behave, be aware of their health and gather detailed knowledge of how they are working is observability.

There are 3 common types of data sources (called telemetry data) that help you uncover the truth:

1. Logs

If you’re a developer, the first thing you add while testing your code is logs. They can be either system generated (e.g. by nginx) or manually generate and can have a variety of data that helps in knowing vital information about the execution of the code.

2. Metrics

They are numerical values quantifying a certain behavioural aspect of your software, which is saved in a time series storage for seeing over a period of time. Most software emit metrics, be it your service running on a pod or the k8s cluster itself.

To put it into context, the throughput (Requests per minute) or Avg response time of your API calls per minute are some metrics that you'll be familiar with and must have noticed in dashboards.

3. Traces

You can think of traces as a specialized form of logs, designed to give details around the set of steps your “request” took. It splits your entire execution into smaller chunks, including code level logic, DB queries & downstream calls. These executions (called “spans”) are easily identifiable with their names and their prefixes. Common names that you might have seen if your team has already setup traces:

  1. Datastore - DB queries and connection handling steps

  2. External - Calls made outside your service over a network
    protocol like HTTP, MQTT etc.

  3. Function - Code execution within the current program There are other span names that can come up based on your

    instrumentation agent.

What is monitoring?

Monitoring is the part where you use the telemetry data to set up dashboards and visualisations of metrics you already know that you need to track to view the system's health at any point in time. Observability means having data such that even when you don’t know what you need to track, you can investigate your system deeply enough.

How does this work?

Below is a sample flow of how observability, when integrated within your micro-services architecture, looks like.

Instrumentation: Refers to how the telemetry data is generated within the system. Typically, it involves adding a small piece of code/program (instrumentation agent) to your existing code.

Ok, but do I need to know how this works? 😬

No. Not really. You can decide to go ahead with a commercial tool and all you need to do is follow a couple of lines of instructions to set it up. All the steps mentioned above are taken care of by them so the details are abstracted out and you can directly start monitoring your system.

Caveat: As your system scales, the cost of the commercial tools will start pinching and you might consider moving to OSS.

Bonus section:

Over the last couple of years, the term o11y is starting to get popular for the word Observability (e.g. The event o11yfest).

Now you say how? Find the output of this code to know how:

def word_encoder(word):
    word = word.replace(" ","") #removing spaces
    mid_char_count = len(word) - 2
    encoding = word[0].lower() + str(mid_char_count) + word[-1].lower()
    print(encoding)
    return encoding

word_encoder("observability")

Are you thinking “where is this inspiration coming from?” Try to find the output for these function calls and you'll know the answer :)

word_encoder("kubernetes")
word_encoder("Andreessen Horowitz")

Fun fact: These words are “numeronym”

If you have come across any other jargon that needs to be simplified, mention them in the comments!

Items.Title: 自然语言处理应用 | Natural Language Processing Applications
Items.Description:
软件开发中心

发表于 4 月 21 日

自然语言是人类交流的主要方法。 它由文字组成，并通过文本、语音和手势来表达。 手势不是这篇文章的主角，所以我们将继续进行文字和演讲。 在我们的数字化世界中，我们非常鼓励与设备进行交流，顺便说一句，阅读这篇文章确实是一种文本交流。 所有这些因素导致了一个事实，即 NLP 已经成为 ML / DS 主题的前沿。 那么我们对 NLP 了解多少？

什么是自然语言处理？

自然语言处理 (NLP) 是人工智能的一个子领域，旨在让计算机像人类一样理解文本和口语。 这个领域对于企业处理来自电子邮件、社交媒体、聊天、调查等的大量数据并从中获取见解非常有价值。NLP 是一种已经在我的许多企业中使用的技术，我们将涵盖其中的一些在下面的部分中。

情绪分析


我们说话的方式常常伴随着使单词的字面意思过于复杂的短语、情感或讽刺。 这些因素在社交媒体中经常遇到，计算机很难理解。 这就是 NLP 主要是其子集 NLU（自然语言理解）的用武之地。 借助 NLU，计算机可以区分情绪并识别文本语气。 情感分析是一种用于识别数据的积极性、消极性或中性的 NLP 方法。 企业经常对文本数据进行情绪分析，以跟踪客户评论中对其品牌和产品的看法，并更好地了解其目标市场。

有几种方法可以将 NLP 用于情感分析任务。 这些都是：

  • 基于规则——使用一些 NLP 技术，例如词干提取、标记化、词性标记。 工作原理如下：定义每组词（负/正），统计每个词组在句子中出现的次数。 根据每个情感组的计数，判断句子是正面的还是负面的。 如果甚至返回中性。 但是基于规则的方法可能会达到一个非常复杂的系统。
  • 自动——这是机器学习实现的地方。 情感分析通常是一个分类问题。 我们已经标记了数据，并试图在给定带有评论/评论/反馈等的句子数据的情况下找到合适的情绪。这种方法的关键方面是合适的。 特征提取和准备特征向量。 为此，我们使用 Bag-of-words、Word Embeddings、TF-IDF。
  • 混合——上述技术的组合。 通常会产生更准确的结果。

请继续关注航空公司情绪分析的实施。

语言翻译


每个人都需要非常快速地翻译一些东西。 无论是谷歌翻译还是任何其他类似的应用程序。 此类应用程序基于称为机器翻译的技术。 它被定义为在不丢失含义的情况下自动将文本从一种语言转换为另一种语言的过程。 与基于规则解决问题的字典之上构建翻译应用程序的时代相比，当前最先进的机器翻译方法 (NMT) 使用神经网络来预测单词的可能性. 与 SMT（统计机器翻译）和基于规则的相比，NMT 的最大优势是速度快，能够根据需要学习单词并重用。

自动校正和预测


今天，有几种可用的程序可以通过检查我们输入的文本的语法和拼写来帮助我们避免电子邮件、消息和其他文档中的拼写和语法错误（例如 Grammarly）。 这些程序包括范围广泛的功能，包括建议同义词、修正语法和拼写错误、为清楚起见而重新措辞，甚至估计用户陈述的隐含语气的能力。 另一个称为“自动预测”的 NLP 功能建议对我们已经开始输入的文本进行自动文本预测。 这有助于用户节省时间并简化他们的工作。 这些是构建此类模型的步骤：

  • 识别拼写错误的单词。
  • 搜索与错误单词相距 N 编辑距离的字符串。
  • 过滤环。
  • 基于单词概率的顺序。
  • 选择最好的情况。 文本预测基于它出现的可能性使用 n-gram。 出现在文档中的连续的单词、标记或其他符号组称为 N-gram。

定向广告


如果您曾经在任何在线商店中搜索任何产品或对象，您会经常在其他网站上看到此类物品和其他相关产品的广告。 这种在线定向广告称为定向广告，是借助自然语言处理进行的。

定向广告的重要组成部分是关键词匹配。 只有那些搜索与广告所附术语相似的术语的人才会看到链接到特定单词或短语的广告。 这显然是不够的； 其他因素，例如他们随后访问的网站和他们看起来感兴趣的网页，也被考虑在内，为他们提供他们可能感兴趣的产品的大量广告。

由于广告只展示给真正对产品感兴趣的客户，这是根据他们的在线行为评估的，因此许多企业从中受益匪浅，并节省了大量资金。

聊天机器人


我个人认为聊天机器人是如今提供在线服务的大多数企业、政府网站不可或缺的一部分。 它们无处不在。 那么，AI 驱动的机器人与基于规则的机器人有何不同？

我们将大多数常见机器人称为“基于规则”的机器人。 他们被要求认真遵守作者制定的对话准则。 当用户输入特定命令时，基于规则的机器人将自动提供预先写好的答案。 然而，如果不遵循这些准则，传统的机器人可能难以为用户提供有价值的信息。 缺乏作为人类对话重要组成部分的适应性。 这些是让 AI 聊天机器人如此特别和有价值的主要因素：

  • 理解自然语言。
  • 沟通的方式不仅仅是问答。
  • 连续的提高。 要训​​练聊天机器人理解自然语言，您需要大量数据。 可以使用包括社交媒体、论坛和客户服务记录在内的众多来源来获取此信息。 为了适应多种情况，数据必须多样化和标记。

作为下一步，您必须预处理收集的数据。 将文本标记为更小的位、清理和规范化数据是执行的关键步骤。

  • 文本规范化——保持数据的一致性，将文本转换为小写，删除标点符号等。
  • 标记化——将文本分解成更小的部分，例如单词或短语。
  • 从文本中删除停用词——如“the”、“is”和“and”等不会显着增加数据集意义的术语。
  • 词形还原——降低数据的维度。
  • 词性标注——识别文本中每个词的语法作用，例如名词、动词或形容词。 那么了解哪种 NLP 技术适合您的业务需求至关重要。 它可以是情感分析、基于机器学习的方法、关键字匹配系统或语言模型。

这篇博文不应该在结束时不提及趋势 ChatGPT。 由 OpenAI 开发的最先进的自然语言处理 (NLP) 模型——ChatGPT 是著名的 GPT-3 (Generative Pertained Transformer 3) 模型的变体，该模型在大量文本数据上进行训练 ( ~ 570GB 的数据集，包括网页、书籍和其他来源）以产生类似人类的回复。 它是机器学习自然语言处理模型的大型语言模型 (LLM) 类的扩展。 随着过去几年计算机能力的提高，这些模型得到了扩展。 随着输入数据集和参数空间变大，LLM 变得更加强大。 语言模型分析数据中的文本以计算后续单词的可能性。 几种概率方法用于训练语言模型。 可训练参数的数量因同一系列模型的成员而异。 相当强大的工具，目前有足够的限制。 期待调查和测试 GPT4……

情感分析——示例代码

让我们尝试对航空公司数据集执行情感分析。

import nltk from nltk.corpus import stopwords from nltk.corpus import wordnet as wn from nltk.stem.wordnet import WordNetLemmatizer

从 nltk.tokenize 导入 word_tokenize


上面我们可以看到主要导入的 Natural Language Toolkit——NLP 和停用词库的集合——不为句子提供任何意义的词集合，因此应该从数据集中删除。

dataset = pd.read_csv("../input/airline/airline_sentiment_analysis.csv")


dataset.info()


检查我们的数据集中没有任何空值。

colors=sns.color_palette() pd.Series(dataset['airline_sentiment']).value_counts().plot(kind='pie',colors=colors,labels=['Negative','Postive'],explode=[0.05,0.05],shadow=True,autopct='%.2f',fontsize=12,figsize=(6,6)) plt.show()


很明显，我们对负面推文的数据不平衡。 因此，我们必须使用处理不平衡数据的技术。 数据集['clean_text']=数据集['text'].apply(lambda x: cleaning_text(x))`
词形还原——消除所有后缀，只留下词根。

词形还原器 = WordNetLemmatizer()

上面的代码片段只是快速简单地清理文本，从文本列中删除停用词、数字和特殊字符。

def convert_Sentiment(sentiment): if sentiment == "positive": return 1 elif sentiment == "negative": return 0

现在我们必须对清理后的文本数据集执行相同的操作。 我们将使用 TF-IDF 向量化器将单词转换为向量，不仅要考虑单词出现的次数，还要考虑该单词的重要性。

tfid = TfidfVectorizer()

现在回到不平衡数据问题，因为我们将其悬而未决。

smote = SMOTE() x_sm,y_sm = smote.fit_resample(X_vect,y)


现在我们已经完成了数据集的准备，我们可以进入训练模型和测试结果的最后步骤。

精度 = accuracy_score(pred,y_test)

打印（'准确度：'+ str（准确度））打印（分类报告（pred，y_test））cm = confusion_matrix（pred，y_test）

监控客户反馈并从中获取情绪有助于企业提高产品和服务质量。 因此，导致更高的销售额。 这个模型会有更多的价值，因为我们可以获得更长时间范围的数据。

我们创造有意义的产品，坚持客户的商业目标。 主要发展领域：数字健康、会计、教育。
